title: Two Instances of Interpretable Neural Network for Universal Approximations

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - TWO INSTANCES OF INTERPRETABLE NEURAL NET WORK FOR UNIVERSAL APPROXIMATIONS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

This paper proposes two bottom-up interpretable neural network (NN) constructions for universal approximation, namely Triangularly-constructed NN (TNN) and Semi-Quantized Activation NN (SQANN). The notable properties are (1) resistance to catastrophic forgetting (2) existence of proof for arbitrarily high accuracies on training dataset (3) for an input _x_ , users can identify specific samples of training data whose activation “fingerprints” are similar to that of _x_ ’s activations. Users can also identify samples that are out of distribution.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Artificial neural networks (NN) have recently seen successful applications in many fields. Modern deep neural network (DNN) architecture, usually trained through the backpropagation mechanism, has been called a black-box because of its lack of interpretability. To tackle this issue, various studies have been performed to understand how a NN works; see the following surveys Arrieta et al. (2020); Gilpin et al. (2018); Tjoa & Guan (2020); Wiegreffe & Marasovi´c (2021). This paper primarily proposes two interpretable models, namely triangularly-constructed NN (TNN) and Semi-Quantized Activation NN (SQANN). Both possess the following three notable properties: (1) Resistance to _catastrophic forgetting_ . (2) Mathematical proofs for arbitrarily high accuracy on training datasets; experimentally demonstrable with python code and simple common datasets (see supp. materials). (3) Detection of out-of-distribution samples through weak activations.
**Concept disambiguation** . Several concepts have multiple possible definitions. We clarify the definitions used in this paper.
1. Interpretability. We consider only fine-grained interpretation, i.e. we look at the meaning of each single neuron or its activation in our models.

2. Universal approximation. Readers might be familiar with universal approximation of functions on certain conditions, e.g. compact sets. Our models can be more general, e.g. user can freely choose the interpolation function between 2 activations based on knowledge of the local manifold. The function can even be pathological. See appendix A.2.1.

3. Catastrophic forgetting: the tendency for knowledge of previously learned dataset to be abruptly lost as information relevant to a new dataset is incorporated. This definition is a slightly nuanced version of Kirkpatrick et al. (2017). Hence, our models’ resistance to catastrophic forgetting is the following. Given a training dataset _D_ accurately modeled by an architecture _M_ , a new dataset _D[′]_ (especially new, out of distribution dataset) can be incorporated into _M_ without losing accuracy on _D_ . See appendix A.2.2 (analogy to biological system included).

**Related works and interpretability issues** . Recent remarkable studies on universal approximators include the Deep Narrow Network by Kidger & Lyons (2020), DeepONet universal approximation for operators by Lu et al. (2021) and the Broad Learning System by Chen et al. (2019); Hanin (2019); Park et al. (2021); Johnson (2019). While insightful, they do not directly address the eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) issue, especially the blackbox property of the DNN. Similarly, a number of classical papers provide theoretical insights for NN as universal approximators, but interpretability, transparency and fairness issues are not their main focus. The universal approximation theorem by Cybenko (1989) asserts that a NN with a single hidden layer can approximate any function to arbitrarily small error under common conditions, proven by asserting the density of that set of NN

in the function space using classic mathematical theorems. In particular, its theorem 1 uses an abstract proof by contradiction. From the proof, it is not easy to observe the internal mechanism of a NN in a straight-forward manner; consequently modern works that depend on it (e.g. Deep Narrow Network) might inherit the blackbox property. Bottom-up constructions for function approximation using NN then emerged, though they also lack the interpretability (see appendix A.3 for more related works). Also consider a demonstration in Nielsen (2015) that could help improve our understanding of universal approximation.

**Outline** . This paper is arranged as the following. Section 2 shows explicit TNN construction, related results, including _a pencil-and-paper example_ for pedagogical purpose. Likewise, section 3 shows SQANN construction, statements regarding SQANN, _another pencil-and-paper example_ , then experimental results of its application, before we conclude the paper with limitations and future works. Python codes and clearer figures are fully available in supp. materials (also see appendix).

## 2 TRIANGULARLY-CONSTRUCTED NN (TNN)

TNN is the prototype NN for our interpretable universal approximator. SQANN (next section) partially borrows the concept from TNN which will be useful as an easy and manageable illustration to deliver the following ideas: (1) organized activations of neurons and (2) the retrieval of _α_ values as the outputs. We start with a simple scalar function _y_ = _f_ ( _x_ ) _∈_ R for _x ∈_ [0 _,_ 1], thus TNN’s interpretability can be illustrated very clearly.

We proceed with SQANN, a universal approximation inspired by TNN that allows multi-dimensional input, multi-layer stacking of neurons based on relative strength of neuron activations.

## 3 SEMI-QUANTIZED ACTIVATION NEURAL NETWORK (SQANN)

SQANN architecture is a multi-dimensional universal approximator which (1) retains TNN’s idea of using an organized sequence of activations to retrieve _α_ , (2) remotely resembles a Radial Basis Function, but (3) has deep neural network properties, such as the possibility of deep learning (multiple constructed layers) and neuron activations. The difference is, a neuron in SQANN corresponds exactly to a data sample as SQANN stores its “fingerprints” as neurons’ nuclei as shown in fig. 3(B). The neurons can be activated with different kinds of responses: (1) distinct peaks and (2) half-activations and (3) weak/zero activations, made possible by double selective activation _σdsa_ . They are illustrated in fig. 3(A) and the sketch of proof of proposition 3 in appendix A.5.4, _intuition behind σdsa_ .

The aforementioned distinct types of activation are key to SQANN’s main result and give the model an interpretability at least in the following sense: samples are highly/moderately/not recognizable if their activation patterns strongly/moderately/weakly resemble the activation patterns of an existing training sample _x_ , where identifications are facilitated by the distinct regions of _σdsa_ . The design is “semi” quantized since _σdsa_ has approximately “distinct” levels yet remains continuous. Since SQANN incorporates multi-layer structure, it avoids being a non-generalizing model that nearest neighbours methods suffer from; see the remark in (Pedregosa et al. (2011a)).

## 3.1 SQANN CONSTRUCTION

**Outline of SQANN construction with interpretations** . SQANN is constructed without optimization like gradient descent. Each indexed training data sample is converted into a “fingerprint” or pattern of neuron activations, which undergoes one of the following:

1. _Admission to layer k_ . Sample’s new/distinct fingerprint is added into layer _k_ if the sample weakly activates existing nodes in the layer ( _∀j,_ [ _vk_ ] _j < τad_ ) and no collision occurs; see fig. 3(D.1).

2. _Collision_ . A sample activates one or more neurons strongly i.e. _∃j, k,_ [ _vk_ ] _j > τact_ . The earliest layer where collision occurs is denoted _lc_ . Such sample is integrated into _lc_ , thus very similar samples are _selectively clustered_ . See fig. 3(D.2); the concept is also illustrated in the sketch of proof for proposition 3, appendix A.5.4.

3. _Filtering into deeper layer_ occurs when neither of the above occurs (no strong activation, some moderate activations). Such sample has features loosely similar to previously seen samples, but we need to filter them further to distinguish its finer features.

At this point, it may be clearer to readers how SQANN is constructed. In short, for each layer, _representative activations_ become the neurons of the layer. In layer 1, representatives activations are the samples themselves. In deeper layers, they are activations propagated to the layer.

Similar to layer 1 construction, perform _check Nk activation_ on ( _u[<i>] , y[<i>]_ ) for _i >_ 1 by computing activation _vk[<i>]_ and checking it against the existing nodes. One of the three cases occur (1) admission, when [ _vk[<i>]_ ] _j < τad_ for all _j_ = 1 _, . . . , nk_ and no collision (2) collision, when there exists index _j_ at a _collided layer lc ≤ k_ such that [ _vl[<i>] c_ ] _j > τact_ or (3) otherwise. If (1) occurs, the activation _vk[<i>]_ is added as a new neuron to the layer, _Nk → concat_ ( _Nk, vk[<i>]_ ). If (3) occurs, filter the data sample for deeper layer. Assuming no collision, the process is repeated for the next unused data sample _u[<i>]_ until all remaining data samples are checked. Once done, repeat the process for layer _k_ + 1 construction.

Suppose collision happens when we check _u[<m>]_ at layer _lc_ , we use the collision resolution mechanism. We destroy all layers _l > lc_ and put the collided sample into _lc_ , i.e. _Nlc → concat_ ( _Nlc , vk[<m>]_ ) and _αlc → concat_ ( _αlc , yk[<m>]_ ) (push-node in the pseudo code). No layer will be destroyed if _lc_ = _k_ , the current layer. The data samples used in each destroyed layer are returned to the list of unused samples in the same order they have been used during the construction (returnindex() in the pseudo code); we refer to this as _order integrity_ . Once the colliding sample is added to _lc_ , effectively, the strong activation in this layer is now overshadowed by maximum activation ( _selective clustering_ in action), since the exact neuron is now included as the representative of itself and its locality. This is possibly a practically inefficient process, since we tear down intermediate layers, but we only prioritize the completion of the construction for now.

Catastrophic forgetting resistance is proven similarly: when new samples are used for training, previous samples are not forgotten since SQANN stores the particular fingerprint _ηl[<k>]_ for each sample.

## 3.2 EXPERIMENT TO TEST GENERALIZABILITY OF SQANN

**Test datasets with increasing spread from training distribution** . The accuracy of SQANN on high-dimensional dataset outside the training dataset is harder to formalize in theorems. Furthermore, real life data is often noisy and possibly not regularly structured. We avoid making any related statements for SQANN for now. We instead provide empirical results on test datasets that are _similar_ to the training dataset, to the extent that each point in the test dataset is a training sample perturbed by uniform random values of increasing magnitude. We refer to the noise magnitude as the _test data spread_ . Fig. 4(A,A.2) show four domains _X_ with different test data spread. Test dataset that has larger test data spread contains data samples that are noisier and further away from the training data points. Fig. 4(B,B.2) show that SQANN naturally performs better with smaller test data spread. As the test data spread increases, larger errors are observed. Likewise, smaller spread means smaller _Ninterp_ , i.e. fewer data samples fail to activate neurons in SQANN strongly. For all, training errors are 0 _as expected from theorem 2_ .

## 4 CONCLUSION, LIMITATION AND FUTURE DIRECTION

**Limitations and future directions** . **TNN** is clearly limited in regards to its application to multidimensional input data although it can be useful on different types of time series data. **SQANN** limitation and possible future development currently include 1) simple sequential drawing of samples that may result in the imbalance of layer size. In the future, more sophisticated ordering of training samples can be used so that layers are constructed with meaningful and purposeful arrangement e.g. deeper layers can be purposefully reserved for rare cases; more research on this is necessary to optimize the results 2) layer destruction during the treatment of collision cases might be an inefficient mechanism, which can be improved in the future.

**Conclusion** , we have proposed TNN and SQANN, two interpretable NNs for universal approximation designed to (1) be resistant to catastrophic forgetting (2) have provably high accuracy on training datasets and (3) can be used to handle out-of-distribution samples.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces two novel neural network architectures, TNN and SQANN, designed for intrinsic interpretability through ordered neuron activations and prototype-based fingerprinting. The authors claim these models provide universal approximation properties while resisting catastrophic forgetting and allowing for explicit out-of-distribution detection.

The paper offers an interesting bottom-up approach to network construction that avoids standard backpropagation. While the interpretability claims are conceptually appealing, the theoretical rigor regarding universal approximation and the empirical validation on real-world data are currently insufficient to support the paper's broad claims.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Formal Complexity Analysis: The paper proposes construction methods for TNN and SQANN that rely on storing individual training samples as 'fingerprints' or neurons. While this ensures perfect training accuracy, the authors fail to provide a formal analysis of the memory and computational complexity as the dataset grows. In Section 5, the collision resolution mechanism involves tearing down and reconstructing layers, which could lead to exponential time complexity in worst-case scenarios. A rigorous discussion on how these models scale to high-dimensional datasets is required to determine if they are practical alternatives to standard architectures.

- Ambiguity in Universal Approximation Proofs: Theorem 1 and Theorem 2 claim universal approximation properties, but the proofs provided in the appendix (referenced in Sections 3 and 5) appear to rely on the ability to perfectly interpolate training points. Universal approximation typically requires showing that a model can approximate any continuous function on a compact set, not just that it can fit a finite set of training points with zero error. The paper needs to clarify whether these architectures can approximate arbitrary continuous functions or if they are simply interpolators that overfit the training set, which would distinguish them from standard universal approximators.

- Incomplete Empirical Evaluation: The experimental results in Section 6 and Table 1 are limited to small datasets like Boston Housing and simple synthetic tasks. The comparison against other regression methods is problematic because the paper uses a custom data augmentation strategy (integrating OOD samples into training) that is not applied to the baselines in a balanced way. To demonstrate the utility of these models, the authors should compare them against established interpretable baselines (e.g., decision trees, GAMs, or prototype-based networks) using standard benchmarks where interpretability is a primary constraint, rather than just showing that adding more data improves performance.

- Fragility of the Collision Resolution Mechanism: The collision resolution mechanism described in Section 5, which triggers layer destruction and reconstruction, is a heuristic that lacks a proof of convergence. Proposition 3 claims the construction completes with high probability, but the argument relies on 'order integrity' without formalizing the conditions on the input distribution under which this holds. If the construction process is sensitive to the order of data presentation, the model's interpretability and stability are fundamentally compromised. The authors must either provide a robust convergence proof or demonstrate empirically that the construction is stable across diverse data orderings.

- Over-reliance on Synthetic Interpretability: The paper claims that structural interpretability inherently leads to human-understandable logic, but this is an assumption rather than a demonstrated fact. Mapping ECG segments to specific neurons in TNN is a promising example, but the paper does not provide a systematic way for a human to interpret the 'fingerprints' stored in deeper SQANN layers. Without a framework to translate these activations into human-readable rules or concepts, the claim of 'fine-grained interpretation' remains speculative. The authors should include a qualitative analysis of what the learned fingerprints actually represent in a non-trivial domain.

- Absence of a formal definition for the interpolation function: The paper relies on a user-defined interpolation function to achieve universal approximation, yet it fails to specify the required properties (such as Lipschitz continuity or boundedness) that this function must satisfy to guarantee convergence to a target function. Without defining the class of admissible interpolation functions, the claim of 'universal approximation' is underspecified. The authors should explicitly state the necessary conditions on the interpolation function and provide a proof or a concrete example showing that, for a standard function like the sine wave, the SQANN construction converges to the target as the density of training samples increases.

- Missing quantitative evidence for OOD detection efficacy: The paper claims that 'weak activations' allow for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, but it provides no formal metric or thresholding analysis to validate this claim. To demonstrate that this mechanism has bite, the authors should perform a standard OOD evaluation, such as measuring the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) curve on a task where the training and test distributions are clearly separated (e.g., training on MNIST digits 0-4 and testing on digits 5-9). This would allow readers to assess whether the proposed weak activation mechanism provides a genuine signal or merely reflects noise in the input space.

- Lack of a scalability demonstration on high-dimensional data: While the paper acknowledges that TNN is limited for multidimensional data, the SQANN architecture is presented as a multi-dimensional approximator without any demonstration on data beyond simple synthetic rings or low-dimensional regression sets. To prove the architecture is more than a toy model, the authors should apply SQANN to a standard high-dimensional benchmark, such as the CIFAR-10 or Fashion-MNIST dataset, and report the memory footprint and construction time. This is necessary to determine if the 'fingerprint' storage approach remains feasible as the input dimensionality and dataset size scale to real-world levels.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a formal computational complexity analysis for the construction of both TNN and SQANN, specifically addressing the worst-case behavior of the collision resolution mechanism.

- Refine the universal approximation theorems to clearly distinguish between fitting finite training data and approximating continuous functions on compact sets.

- Conduct a more rigorous comparative study against established interpretable baselines on standard datasets, ensuring that the data augmentation strategy is applied consistently across all models.

- Add a section or appendix detailing the stability of the construction process under different data permutations to address the concerns regarding the collision resolution heuristic.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Universal Approximation
comments.quote: This paper proposes two bottom-up interpretable neural network (NN) constructions for universal approximation, namely Triangularly-constructed NN (TNN) and Semi-Quantized Activation NN (SQANN).
comments.explanation: The paper conflates 'universal approximation' with the ability to achieve zero training error. Universal approximation requires showing that a model class can approximate any continuous function on a compact set as the number of parameters increases. The proposed models are exact interpolators that store training samples as 'fingerprints,' which is a distinct property from universal approximation. Please revise the terminology throughout the paper to describe these models as exact interpolators or memory-based architectures, as the current claim is not supported by the provided proofs.

comments.title: Biased Evaluation of OOD Detection
comments.quote: t samples. (3) SQANN's activations are used to collect samples with large absolute errors  $e_{\tau}(x) = |SQANN(x) - y_0| > \tau$  and we treat them as out-of-distribution (OOD) samples. These samples are considered as new distinct samples to be integrated into  $D$  as the new training dataset  $D'$ .
comments.explanation: The methodology for comparing SQANN against baselines is biased because the augmented training set D' is derived using SQANN's internal activation mechanism. This provides an information advantage to SQANN that is not available to the other models, which are simply retrained on samples that happened to be difficult for SQANN. To demonstrate that the OOD detection has genuine utility, you must use a model-agnostic method (e.g., density estimation) to select D' or report performance for all models using a common, externally defined augmented set.

comments.title: Dimensional Inconsistency in Collision Resolution
comments.quote: Suppose collision happens when we check $u^{<m>}$ at layer $l_{c}$, we use the collision resolution mechanism. We destroy all layers $l>l_{c}$ and put the collided sample into $l_{c}$, i.e. $N_{l_{c}}\rightarrow concat(N_{l_{c}},v_{k}^{<m>})$ and $\alpha_{l_{c}}\rightarrow concat(\alpha_{l_{c}},y_{k}^{<m>})$
comments.explanation: The collision resolution mechanism is mathematically ill-defined. It attempts to concatenate an activation vector v_k^{<m>} from layer k into the set of neurons N_{l_c} in layer l_c. Since neurons in layer l_c are vectors of dimension n_{l_c} and v_k^{<m>} has dimension n_k, this operation is only valid if n_k = n_{l_c}, which is not guaranteed. Please define a projection operator or mapping function to reconcile these dimensions before concatenation.

comments.title: Dimensional Mismatch in Layer-to-Layer Activation
comments.quote: In SQANN, activations will be forwarded layer by layer, i.e.  $[v_1]_j = \sigma_{dsa}\big(\|x - \eta_1^{<j>}\|\big)$  where  $\eta_1^{<j>} \in N_1$  and  $[v_{k+1}]_j = \sigma_{dsa}\big(\|v_k - \eta_{k+1}^{<j>}\|\big)$
comments.explanation: The definition of the activation for layer k+1 is dimensionally inconsistent. The input v_k is a vector of dimension n_k, so the fingerprint \eta_{k+1}^{<j>} must also be a vector of dimension n_k for the norm to be well-defined. The current notation conflates the activation vector v_{k+1} with the set of fingerprints N_{k+1}. Please clarify the dimensionality of the fingerprints in deeper layers and ensure the norm is well-defined by explicitly stating that \eta_{k+1}^{<j>} \in R^{n_k}$.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes two novel neural network constructions, TNN and SQANN, that explicitly map neurons to training samples to enable direct activation-based interpretation. The architectural design avoids gradient-based training, which sidesteps certain optimization issues and allows for straightforward analytical construction. The focus on interpretable, sample-matching mechanisms offers a fresh perspective on prototype-based models and incremental learning.

Overview issues:

- Conflation of Universal Approximation with Finite-Dataset Interpolation: The paper repeatedly claims its architectures achieve "universal approximation," yet Theorems 1 and 2 only prove zero error on the finite training set. The proofs rely on constructing parameters that exactly match training targets, which is a memorization guarantee. Standard universal approximation results bound the error over continuous function classes as network capacity grows. By focusing solely on training interpolation, the authors sidestep the core mathematical challenge of generalization. This misleads readers about the theoretical contribution. The authors should reframe their claims to "perfect interpolation" or provide actual approximation error bounds for continuous functions on compact domains.

- Lack of Quantitative Interpretability Metrics and Validation: The authors define interpretability as examining the meaning of single neurons and claim that mapping neurons to training samples constitutes fine-grained interpretation. The paper provides no standardized metrics to measure this interpretability. Equating architectural transparency with actual interpretability is a common pitfall. Without user studies or fidelity metrics, the claimed interpretability remains an unverified assumption. This matters because the core selling point of the paper is its interpretability, yet the evaluation lacks the empirical rigor expected in modern XAI research. The authors should adopt established interpretability metrics or conduct a user study to validate that the activation fingerprints actually aid human understanding.

- Misapplication of Catastrophic Forgetting in a Non-Gradient Framework: The authors claim resistance to catastrophic forgetting and reference Kirkpatrick et al. (2017). This claim is fundamentally misplaced because catastrophic forgetting arises in fixed-architecture networks trained via gradient-based weight updates. SQANN and TNN are constructed explicitly without backpropagation. New samples are simply added as new neurons. Avoiding forgetting by expanding the model architecture is trivial and does not address the actual problem of continual learning. This misrepresents the contribution and fails to engage with the real challenges of continual learning. The authors should either remove the catastrophic forgetting claim or reframe the work as an incremental learning method that trades parameter efficiency for memorization capacity.

- Flawed Experimental Setup and Unfair Baseline Comparisons: The experimental evaluation relies on a non-standard protocol that weakens the results. For the regression experiments, the authors train on only 20% of the data, then retrain all models after adding a large fraction of the dataset back into the training set. This setup heavily favors models that can easily incorporate new data and makes the comparison of generalization performance meaningless. Furthermore, the test on synthetic data only varies the spread of uniform noise. This does not reflect real-world distribution shifts. The results do not demonstrate the model's ability to generalize to unseen data under standard conditions. The authors should evaluate all models on a fixed, held-out test set without iterative retraining.

- Unaddressed Computational and Structural Complexity of SQANN: The SQANN construction algorithm involves sequential sample processing, collision detection, and the destruction of deeper layers when collisions occur. The paper acknowledges this might be inefficient but provides no analysis of time or space complexity. The reliance on arbitrary thresholds and the "order integrity" mechanism make the architecture highly sensitive to data ordering. This matters because a model that requires tearing down intermediate layers and lacks scalability guarantees is impractical for real-world applications. The authors should provide a complexity analysis and demonstrate the method on a moderately sized dataset to show its practical feasibility.

- Worked example for pathological function approximation claim: The abstract and introduction assert that the architectures generalize beyond compact sets and can approximate pathological functions via user-defined interpolation. The current proofs and experiments only demonstrate exact interpolation on finite training sets, leaving this claim unverified. Readers will wonder how the mechanism behaves when the underlying function lacks smoothness or has unbounded support. The authors should compute the approximation of a standard pathological function, such as the Weierstrass function or a nowhere-differentiable Weierstrass-type function, using their explicit construction. Showing the activation patterns and interpolation error for this specific DGP would prove the result has bite and clarify the practical limits of the claim.

- Comparison against established interpretable baselines: The experimental evaluation pits SQANN against standard black-box regressors like Ridge, Lasso, and MLP, but never compares it to transparent, constructive alternatives. This omission obscures whether the proposed activation fingerprints and layer construction actually improve upon well-known interpretable methods. The paper would be significantly stronger if it benchmarked against Radial Basis Function (RBF) networks, k-Nearest Neighbors, or other explicit constructive approximators. Running the Boston Housing and Diabetes experiments with these baselines would clarify the trade-offs between sample efficiency, approximation accuracy, and interpretability that the authors claim to deliver.

- Implementation discussion for the user-defined interpolation function: The authors repeatedly state that users can freely choose the interpolation function between activations based on local manifold knowledge, yet they provide no practical guidance on how to select or tune this choice. The text briefly gives a weighted average formula but omits any discussion of how to estimate the local manifold or how different interpolation choices affect approximation error. A natural question is whether the curse of dimensionality makes manifold estimation infeasible for the high-dimensional inputs SQANN claims to handle. The authors should add a subsection or appendix detailing how to parameterize the interpolation kernel, how to estimate local geometry from the activation fingerprints, and what convergence rates to expect.

- Detailed step-by-step walkthrough for SQANN construction: The SQANN construction algorithm involves sequential sample processing, collision detection, thresholding, and the destruction of deeper layers, but the provided pencil-and-paper example is too brief to trace these mechanics. Readers will struggle to understand how layer sizes evolve or how the order-integrity mechanism prevents infinite cycling without a complete trace. The authors should include a fully-specified, step-by-step walkthrough on a standard small dataset like XOR or a simple quadratic surface. Walking through the admission, collision, and filtering steps for each sample would let readers verify the mechanism and calibrate their intuition about the architecture's behavior.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper's core theoretical claims misrepresent standard universal approximation results, and the experimental design fails to evaluate generalization under standard conditions. Addressing the interpretability metrics, computational complexity, and baseline comparisons requires substantial restructuring and additional experiments.

Revision targets:

- Reframe the theoretical contributions to focus on exact interpolation rather than universal approximation, and provide approximation error bounds for continuous functions if the original claim is to be maintained.

- Replace the iterative retraining experimental protocol with a standard fixed-test-set evaluation to properly benchmark generalization and OOD detection capabilities.

- Introduce quantitative interpretability metrics or a user study to validate the claimed fine-grained interpretability of the activation fingerprints.

- Provide a formal time and space complexity analysis for SQANN construction and discuss the impact of data ordering and collision thresholds on scalability.

comments.title: Conflation of interpolation with universal approximation
comments.quote: This paper proposes two bottom-up interpretable neural network (NN) constructions for universal approximation, namely Triangularly-constructed NN (TNN) and Semi-Quantized Activation NN (SQANN). The notable properties are (1) resistance to catastrophic forgetting (2) existence of proof for arbitrarily high accuracies on training dataset
comments.explanation: The abstract claims these architectures achieve universal approximation, yet the second listed property explicitly limits the guarantee to arbitrarily high accuracies on the training dataset. Universal approximation theorems bound the error over continuous function classes on compact domains as network capacity grows, whereas exact interpolation on a finite set is a memorization guarantee. By equating finite-sample interpolation with universal approximation, the abstract misrepresents the theoretical scope of the work. The authors should distinguish between interpolation guarantees and approximation bounds, or reframe the contribution as exact interpolation rather than universal approximation.

comments.title: Misapplication of catastrophic forgetting
comments.quote: The notable properties are (1) resistance to catastrophic forgetting
comments.explanation: Catastrophic forgetting refers to the degradation of previously learned representations when a fixed-architecture network undergoes gradient-based updates on new data. The proposed models avoid this issue entirely by expanding the architecture with new neurons for new samples, which is a trivial consequence of their bottom-up construction rather than a novel resistance to forgetting. Claiming this as a notable property misleads readers about the continual learning problem, which assumes a fixed parameter budget. The authors should remove this claim or explicitly state that the model trades parameter efficiency for incremental capacity.

comments.title: Index reversal in alpha computation matrix
comments.quote: )_{1}$. Putting them together as $y=[y^{(1)},\ldots,y^{(N)}]^{T}$, we get $y=A\alpha$ where $A$ is an upper-left triangular matrix and the inverse $A^{-1}$ exists. Thus, $\alpha=A^{-1}y$, a matrix such that $A^{-1}_{ij}=1$ along the diagonal, $A^{-1}_{i,i+1}=-1$ and zeroes otherwise, which facilitates a convenient computation. The triangular const
comments.explanation: The described inverse matrix $A^{-1}$ (1s on diagonal, -1s on superdiagonal) computes $\alpha_i = y^{(i)} - y^{(i+1)}$ for $i<N$ and $\alpha_N = y^{(N)}$. However, the system $y=A\alpha$ derived from the activation pattern implies $y^{(1)} = \sum_{j=1}^N \alpha_j$, $y^{(2)} = \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} \alpha_j$, ..., $y^{(N)} = \alpha_1$. Solving this yields $\alpha_N = y^{(1)} - y^{(2)}$, $\alpha_{N-1} = y^{(2)} - y^{(3)}$, ..., $\alpha_1 = y^{(N)}$. The matrix $A^{-1}$ as written produces the correct differences but in reverse order, meaning it outputs $[\alpha_N, \alpha_{N-1}, \dots, \alpha_1]^T$ instead of $[\alpha_1, \dots, \alpha_N]^T$. The authors should explicitly state that the matrix yields $\alpha$ in reverse index order, or redefine the indexing of $\alpha$ and $y$ to align with the matrix multiplication.

comments.title: Undefined scale parameters in SQANN activation function
comments.quote:
on. Given selective activation $\pi(x,a)=\frac{a}{a+x^{2}}$ and Super Gaussian $s_{g}(x,a)=exp(-(x/a)^{2n})$, $n=4$,$r=0.5$, then the double selective activation is (fig. 3(A)):

$\sigma_{dsa}(x,a_{1},a_{2},r)=(1-r)\times\pi(x,a_{1})+r\times s_{g}(x,a_{2})$ (4)

Nodes activation. Denote the “synapse” or the activation value of node $j$ at layer $k$ by input $v$ as:

$[v_{k}]_{j}=\sigma_{dsa}\big{(}||v-\eta_{k}^{<j>}||\big{)}$ (5)

<!-- PAGE B
comments.explanation: Equation (4) defines $\sigma_{dsa}$ as a function of four arguments $(x, a_1, a_2, r)$, explicitly fixing $n=4$ and $r=0.5$. Equation (5) evaluates $\sigma_{dsa}$ using only the distance $||v-\eta_k^{<j>}||$, completely omitting the scale parameters $a_1$ and $a_2$. Without specifying how $a_1$ and $a_2$ are determined (e.g., as fixed constants, layer-dependent values, or learned parameters), the activation function is mathematically ill-defined. Furthermore, the claimed activation behaviors ("distinct peaks and half-activations") depend critically on the ratio and magnitude of $a_1$ and $a_2$. The authors must specify how these scale parameters are passed to or computed within Equation (5).

comments.title: Missing output aggregation mechanism for prediction claim
comments.quote: We create the SQANN model that predicts $y^{(k)}=SQANN(x^{(k)})$ with provably perfect accuracy and generalizes well to similar test distribution.
comments.explanation: The claim of "provably perfect accuracy" and the definition of the prediction function $SQANN(x^{(k)})$ lack a crucial component: the mechanism for combining layer activations into a final output. The text defines how activations $[v_k]_j$ are computed and notes that $\alpha_k$ stores output values $y_k^{<j>}$, but it never specifies how these stored values are aggregated (e.g., weighted sum, max pooling, attention) to produce the final prediction. Without this aggregation rule, the claim of perfect interpolation on the training set cannot be verified, nor can the generalization behavior be analyzed. The authors should add the explicit prediction formula after the layer propagation description.

comments.title: Arbitrary dummy point affects left boundary
comments.quote: For $\Delta^{(N)}$, we need a dummy $x^{(N+1)}$ value or we can directly choose its value, e.g. $\frac{1}{N}\Sigma_{k=1}^{N-1}\Delta^{(k)}$. The effect is illustrated by the value near $x=0$ in fig. 2(A1-3) and should not pose any problem; the chosen dummy value will only affect the shape at the left end of the graph.
comments.explanation: The choice of a dummy $x^{(N+1)}$ directly determines $W_1$ and $b_1$ via $W_1 = 2a/\Delta^{(N)}$ and $b_1 = a - W_1 x^{(N)}$. Since neuron 1 is active for all $x \in [0,1]$ (its activation transitions at $x^{(N)}$, the smallest training point), $W_1$ and $b_1$ influence the baseline output across the entire domain. Arbitrarily choosing $\Delta^{(N)}$ scales the contribution of $\alpha_1$ globally, not just at the left end. This can introduce significant systematic bias or distortion across the whole approximation, contradicting the claim that it "should not pose any problem." The authors should justify why the global impact of $\alpha_1$ is negligible or provide a principled method for selecting $\Delta^{(N)}$ that minimizes global error.

comments.title: Contradiction between reported MSE improvements
comments.quote: itting). From Boston dataset: for  $\tau = 5$ , SQANN MSE improved from 9.90 to 3.08. Decision tree improves the most with  $D'$  (7.36 to 2.07); see table 1.
comments.explanation: The numerical values provided directly contradict the textual claim. SQANN's MSE drops by 6.82 (9.90 to 3.08), while the Decision Tree's MSE drops by 5.29 (7.36 to 2.07). SQANN shows the larger absolute improvement. The authors should correct the text to accurately reflect the table values or verify the reported numbers.

comments.title: Unverified claim of zero test error on perturbed data
comments.quote: With activation parameters $a_{1}=a_{2}=1$, we achieve zero error not only for training dataset (to be expected from theorem 2) but also on test dataset.
comments.explanation: Achieving zero classification error on perturbed test points is only possible if the perturbation magnitude is smaller than the margin between the decision boundaries. The text does not specify the perturbation radius or the margin width. Clarify whether the perturbation is small enough to preserve class labels, or if the zero-error claim refers to the unperturbed test set.

comments.title: Decision tree performance claim lacks empirical support
comments.quote: With  $\tau = 2$ , regression performance of SQANN improves greatly compared to other methods, except for decision tree regression. We have thus also seen that SQANN can be used to perform sample selections for data that appear to be out of distribution; this has improved decision tree performance greatly.
comments.explanation: The discussion claims that using SQANN for sample selection improved decision tree performance, but Table 1 only reports the MSE of the models themselves (including SQANN) after retraining on expanded datasets. It does not report the performance of a decision tree trained on the samples selected by SQANN, nor does it provide a comparison showing DT's performance before and after SQANN-based selection. The claim is therefore not supported by the provided empirical results. The authors should add a row to Table 1 showing the decision tree's performance when trained on the samples selected by SQANN.

comments.title: Undefined notation in error threshold formula
comments.quote: SQANN's activations are used to collect samples with large absolute errors  $e_{\tau}(x) = |SQANN(x) - y_0| > \tau$  and we treat them as out-of-distribution (OOD) samples.
comments.explanation: The symbol $y_0$ appears in the error formula but is never defined in this section or the surrounding text. It should represent the true target value for input $x$, typically denoted as $y$ or $y^{(i)}$. This notation error obscures the definition of the error threshold used to select OOD samples. The authors should rewrite the equation as $e_{\tau}(x) = |SQANN(x) - y| > \tau$ and explicitly state that $y$ is the ground-truth label.

comments.title: Incorrect remark about overfitting on held-out data
comments.quote: (5) Then MSE is measured again on  $D_{test}$  (yes, there will be partial overfitting).
comments.explanation: $D_{test}$ is defined in step (2) as the "unused data" held out during the initial training. Since $D_{test}$ is disjoint from both the initial training set $D$ and the newly added OOD samples in $D'$, retraining on $D'$ does not cause overfitting to $D_{test}$. Measuring performance on a held-out set is standard evaluation, not overfitting. The authors should remove the parenthetical remark to avoid confusing the reader about the evaluation protocol.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of deeper layer filtering purpose
comments.quote: e.g. deeper layers can be purposefully reserved for rare cases
comments.explanation: The suggestion that deeper layers are reserved for 'rare cases' contradicts the SQANN construction logic. Section 3.1 defines filtering into deeper layers as occurring when a sample exhibits 'no strong activation, some moderate activations,' indicating loose similarity to existing data rather than rarity. Rare or highly distinct samples would typically activate a newly created node at a shallower layer or be handled as outliers. This phrasing misleads readers about the architectural intent. The authors should rewrite this to accurately reflect the admission and filtering criteria.

comments.title: Contradiction between mathematical claim and empirical dataset use
comments.quote:
paper introduces function approximators with novel properties. It is purely mathematical and algorithmic. No specific ethical issues are present. The ethical context depends only on the dataset but in this paper, only common public datasets have been used.

# REPRODUCIBILITY
comments.explanation: The claim that the work is 'purely mathematical and algorithmic' directly conflicts with the empirical evaluation using real-world datasets like Boston Housing and Diabetes. Purely mathematical papers do not typically involve dataset selection or empirical testing. Furthermore, dismissing ethical concerns solely based on the use of 'common public datasets' overlooks well-documented biases in those specific datasets. The authors should either remove the 'purely mathematical' characterization to accurately reflect the empirical nature of the work, or clarify how the ethical implications of dataset usage are mitigated in this approximation context.

comments.title: Contradictory phrasing and unsupported reproducibility claim
comments.quote: Results are easily reproducible even with without random number seeding since data distributions are sufficiently controlled.
comments.explanation: The justification that data distributions are sufficiently controlled does not logically guarantee reproducibility without seeding. Controlled data distributions only affect the input samples, not stochastic elements like weight initialization, data shuffling, or sampling procedures during construction. If the proposed architectures and training procedures are fully deterministic, the authors should state that explicitly. Otherwise, the claim should be qualified to specify which sources of randomness are eliminated. The phrase "with without" also creates unnecessary ambiguity and should be resolved to match the intended meaning.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes two interpretable neural network architectures (TNN and SQANN) with claims of universal approximation, resistance to catastrophic forgetting, and out-of-distribution detection. While the ideas are interesting, the paper suffers from several critical issues: the theoretical claims are overstated relative to what is actually proven, the empirical evaluation is weak and does not support the claimed advantages, the scope of the contributions is unclear, and the writing is often imprecise or contradictory.

The paper presents novel bottom-up constructions for interpretable neural networks, which is a worthwhile goal. However, the main theoretical result (Theorem 1) is essentially a restatement of the construction, not a proof of universal approximation in the standard sense. The empirical evaluation is minimal and does not convincingly demonstrate the claimed advantages over existing methods. The paper reads more like a technical report or extended abstract than a mature conference paper.

Overview issues:

- Overstated theoretical claims: The paper claims 'universal approximation' for both TNN and SQANN, but the proofs provided (Theorem 1 for TNN, Theorem 2 for SQANN) only show that the models can exactly fit the training data points, not that they can approximate arbitrary functions to any desired accuracy on a continuous domain. Theorem 1 states 'TNN achieves arbitrarily high accuracy on the training dataset' – this is trivial for any model that interpolates the data points. The standard universal approximation theorem (Cybenko 1989) is about approximating any continuous function on a compact set, not just fitting a finite set of points. The paper conflates interpolation with universal approximation. The authors need to either clarify that they are proving exact interpolation (not universal approximation in the standard sense) or provide a genuine universal approximation result with appropriate error bounds on the function space.

- Catastrophic forgetting claim is unsupported: The paper claims that TNN and SQANN are 'resistant to catastrophic forgetting' but provides no rigorous definition or proof of this property. The argument in Section 2 (Proposition 1) is that when new out-of-distribution samples are added, old samples are still identified with their neurons and thus 'still approximated accurately.' However, this ignores the fact that adding new neurons changes the network's output function globally, potentially affecting predictions for old samples even if their corresponding neurons remain. The paper does not analyze how the interpolation behavior changes when new neurons are added, nor does it provide any experimental evidence of resistance to forgetting (e.g., comparing performance on old tasks after sequential training). The claim is essentially that the model is a lookup table with interpolation, which is not a meaningful form of catastrophic forgetting resistance. The authors should either provide a formal proof of forgetting resistance or significantly weaken their claims.

- SQANN construction may not complete deterministically: Proposition 3 states that SQANN construction 'completes with high probability p ≈ 1', but the proof is relegated to an appendix that is not included in the reviewed text. The construction involves tearing down and rebuilding layers when collisions occur, and it is not obvious that this process terminates, especially for large datasets or pathological orderings. The paper acknowledges that 'this is possibly a practically inefficient process' but does not analyze the computational complexity or provide termination guarantees. Without a clear proof of termination (or at least an upper bound on the number of iterations), the practical feasibility of SQANN is questionable. The authors should provide a termination proof or discuss conditions under which the construction is guaranteed to complete.

- Empirical evaluation is insufficient: The experimental section is minimal and does not convincingly demonstrate the claimed advantages. For TNN, no experiments are shown at all – only a pencil-and-paper example. For SQANN, the experiments are limited to: (1) a toy classification problem with 4 training points, (2) a regression experiment on Boston Housing and Diabetes datasets where the model is iteratively retrained on test samples (which is essentially data leakage). The comparison with other methods is flawed because SQANN is given access to test labels during the retraining phase, while other methods are not given this advantage in a fair way. The paper claims that SQANN can detect out-of-distribution samples, but the only evidence is that samples with large errors are selected for retraining – this is circular because the model's own errors define what is 'out-of-distribution.' A proper evaluation would include: (a) standard train/test splits without retraining on test data, (b) comparison with interpretable baselines (e.g., k-nearest neighbors, decision trees, splines), (c) metrics for interpretability (e.g., how well can users identify influential training samples?), and (d) a demonstration of catastrophic forgetting resistance using standard benchmarks (e.g., permuted MNIST, split CIFAR).

- Interpretability claims are vague and unvalidated: The paper claims that TNN and SQANN are 'interpretable' because users can identify which training samples have similar activation patterns to a given input. However, no user study or quantitative interpretability metric is provided. The claim that 'users can identify specific samples of training data whose activation fingerprints are similar to that of x's activations' is essentially a description of nearest-neighbor retrieval, which is not novel. The paper does not demonstrate that this form of interpretability provides any practical benefit over simpler methods (e.g., k-nearest neighbors with Euclidean distance). Moreover, the interpretability of deeper layers in SQANN is questionable because the 'fingerprints' stored in deeper layers are activations from previous layers, not the original input features – it is unclear how a user would interpret these. The authors should either provide a rigorous definition of interpretability with validation (e.g., user studies, case studies) or temper their claims.

- TNN is limited to 1D inputs and monotonic ordering: TNN is constructed for scalar inputs x ∈ [0,1] with a strict linear ordering assumption. The paper acknowledges this limitation but does not provide a meaningful extension to multi-dimensional inputs. The claim that TNN 'can be useful on different types of time series data' is speculative. For multi-dimensional data, the paper proposes SQANN, but SQANN is fundamentally different from TNN (it uses radial basis-like activations rather than the triangular construction). The paper does not explain how the two architectures relate or why both are needed. A more coherent contribution would focus on one architecture and develop it thoroughly, or clearly delineate the scope of each.

- Missing demonstration of universal approximation on continuous functions: The paper claims universal approximation for both TNN and SQANN, but the proofs only show exact interpolation of a finite set of training points. For TNN, Theorem 1 states 'arbitrarily high accuracy on the training dataset,' which is trivial for any interpolating model. The standard universal approximation theorem (Cybenko 1989) concerns approximating any continuous function on a compact set to arbitrary precision. The paper should either compute the approximation error for a concrete continuous function (e.g., f(x)=sin(2πx) on [0,1]) using TNN with increasing N and a, showing that the error decays to zero, or clarify that the result is about interpolation, not universal approximation in the usual sense. Without this, the central claim of the paper is misleading.

- No simulation or experiment for TNN on a realistic dataset: The paper provides only a pencil-and-paper example with three points for TNN and no experimental results. For a paper claiming a new neural network architecture, this is a major gap. The authors should include a simulation on a standard 1D regression dataset (e.g., the 'sinc' function with added noise) showing TNN's training and test error, and compare to a standard MLP or spline. The paper mentions ECG signals but provides no such experiment. Without this, the practical utility of TNN is unsubstantiated.

- Catastrophic forgetting claim lacks formal definition and demonstration: The paper claims resistance to catastrophic forgetting but provides no formal definition or proof. The argument that old samples remain accurately approximated because their neurons persist ignores that adding new neurons changes the output function globally. The paper should provide a standard benchmark (e.g., permuted MNIST or split CIFAR) comparing TNN/SQANN to baselines like elastic weight consolidation (EWC) or simple fine-tuning, measuring accuracy on old tasks after sequential training. Without this, the claim is unsupported and likely false in the standard sense.

- SQANN construction termination guarantee is unsubstantiated: Proposition 3 claims SQANN construction completes with high probability p≈1, but the proof is relegated to an appendix that is not included in the reviewed text. The construction involves tearing down and rebuilding layers when collisions occur, and it is not obvious that this process terminates, especially for large datasets. The paper should provide a clear termination proof or at least an upper bound on the number of iterations, and discuss the computational complexity. Without this, the practical feasibility of SQANN is questionable.

Recommendation: reject

Revision targets:

- Clarify the theoretical claims: distinguish between exact interpolation of finite training points and universal approximation of functions on continuous domains. Provide a genuine universal approximation result with error bounds, or rename the contribution accordingly.

- Provide a rigorous definition and proof of catastrophic forgetting resistance, or remove the claim. If the claim is retained, include experiments on standard continual learning benchmarks.

- Significantly expand the empirical evaluation with proper train/test splits, comparison to relevant baselines (including k-NN, decision trees, spline-based models), and metrics for interpretability.

- Address the termination guarantee for SQANN construction with a proof or clear conditions under which it terminates.

- Rewrite the paper with clearer organization, precise definitions, and consistent notation. The current draft reads as a collection of ideas rather than a coherent paper.

comments.title: Universal approximation conflated with interpolation
comments.quote: Our models can be more general, e.g. user can freely choose the interpolation function between 2 activations based on knowledge of the local manifold. The function can even be pathological. See appendix A.2.1.
comments.explanation: The paper's claim of 'universal approximation' is ambiguous. The standard definition (Cybenko, Hornik) requires approximation of arbitrary functions on a continuous domain, not just exact fit at training points. The construction only guarantees exact interpolation at training points; behavior between points depends on the chosen interpolation function, which may not approximate the true function. The claim that 'the function can even be pathological' is misleading because the model only fits training data, not the entire function. The authors should clarify that they prove exact interpolation of finite datasets, not universal approximation in the standard sense. Add a clarification after the quoted text: 'Note that this construction guarantees exact fit only at the training points; the behavior between points is determined by the chosen interpolation function and does not necessarily approximate the underlying function on the continuous domain. This is distinct from the classical universal approximation property.'

comments.title: Catastrophic forgetting claim lacks formal support
comments.quote: Conclusion, we have proposed TNN and SQANN, two interpretable NNs for universal approximation designed to (1) be resistant to catastrophic forgetting (2) have provably high accuracy on training datasets and (3) can be used to handle out-of-distribution samples.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims that TNN and SQANN are 'resistant to catastrophic forgetting,' but the paper provides no rigorous definition or proof of this property. The argument in Section 2 (Proposition 1) is that when new out-of-distribution samples are added, old samples are still identified with their neurons and thus 'still approximated accurately.' However, this ignores that adding new neurons changes the network's output function globally, potentially affecting predictions for old samples. The paper does not analyze how interpolation behavior changes when new neurons are added, nor does it provide any experimental evidence of resistance to forgetting. The claim is essentially that the model is a lookup table with interpolation, which is not a meaningful form of catastrophic forgetting resistance. The authors should either provide a formal proof of forgetting resistance or significantly weaken their claims. Remove the phrase 'be resistant to catastrophic forgetting' or replace it with a more modest claim such as 'can incorporate new training samples without discarding old ones.'

comments.title: Theorem 1 conflates interpolation with universal approximation
comments.quote:
Theorem 1

TNN achieves arbitrarily high accuracy on the training dataset. Proof: see appendix A.4.1. Also see example results in fig. 2.
comments.explanation: The theorem statement only guarantees arbitrarily high accuracy on the finite training set, which is a property of interpolation (e.g., a lookup table or polynomial interpolation). The surrounding text (just before the theorem) says 'rigorous proof on universal approximation at work,' but the theorem does not address approximation of any continuous function on a compact domain, which is the standard meaning of universal approximation (Cybenko 1989). The paper should either rename Theorem 1 to 'TNN exactly interpolates the training dataset' and remove the phrase 'universal approximation at work,' or provide a genuine universal approximation result with error bounds on a continuous domain. Rewrite Theorem 1 as 'TNN exactly interpolates the training dataset' and remove the phrase 'universal approximation at work' from the preceding sentence.

comments.title: Proposition 1 error bound is not justified
comments.quote:
Proposition 1

Errors on monotonous interval. Given finite training, test datasets $D,D^{\prime}$, there exists $A\subseteq D^{\prime}$ such that, using TNN constructed with $D\cup A$, for all samples in test dataset $(x^{\prime},y^{\prime})\in D^{\prime}$, sample-wise error $e=|y^{\prime}-TNN(x^{\prime})|$ has an upper bound $max(|y^{\prime}-y^{(k+1)}|,|y^{\prime}-y^{(k)}|)$ for some $k$. Proof: see appendix A.4.2.
comments.explanation: The proposition's bound depends on an undefined index 'k' — it is unclear which training samples y^{(k)} and y^{(k+1)} are referenced. Moreover, the bound max(|y' - y^{(k+1)}|, |y' - y^{(k)}|) is essentially the distance to the nearest training labels, which is trivial and does not exploit any property of TNN beyond what a nearest-neighbor method would provide. The proof is relegated to an appendix not included in the reviewed text, so the logical chain cannot be verified. The authors should either provide a non-trivial error bound that uses TNN's specific structure or remove this proposition.

comments.title: Mid-point property holds only in the limit a→∞
comments.quote:
Proposition 2

Mid-point property. The mid-point $x_{mid,k}=\frac{1}{2}(x^{(k)}+x^{(k+1)})$ takes the value of $\alpha^{T}\sigma(Wx_{mid,k}+b)=\frac{1}{2}(y^{(k)}+y^{(k+1)})$. Proof: see appendix A.4.3.
comments.explanation: The mid-point property is a direct consequence of the linear interpolation inherent in the triangular construction when a→∞ (exact step function activations). However, for finite a, the activation at the mid-point is not exactly 0.5 for the relevant neuron due to the sigmoid's nonlinearity — equation (2) only guarantees that (Wx^{(k)}+b)_j is either ≥a or ≤-a at the training points, but at the mid-point the weighted sum may not be exactly 0, so σ may not be exactly 0.5. The proposition as stated claims exact equality for any a, which is false unless a→∞. The proof is relegated to an appendix not included in the reviewed text, so the conditions under which this holds cannot be verified. The authors should either state that the property holds exactly only in the limit a→∞, or provide a proof that works for finite a.

comments.title: Construction of W and b for Δ^{(N)} is ambiguous
comments.quote: For $\Delta^{(N)}$, we need a dummy $x^{(N+1)}$ value or we can directly choose its value, e.g. $\frac{1}{N}\Sigma_{k=1}^{N-1}\Delta^{(k)}$. The effect is illustrated by the value near $x=0$ in fig. 2(A1-3) and should not pose any problem; the chosen dummy value will only affect the shape at the left end of the graph.
comments.explanation: The construction of W and b for the last neuron (k=N) requires a dummy x^{(N+1)} or an arbitrary choice for Δ^{(N)}. However, the paper does not specify how this choice affects the ordered activation property (equation (1)) for the last neuron. If Δ^{(N)} is chosen arbitrarily, the condition (Wx^{(N)}+b)_1 = a may not hold, violating the desired activation pattern. The paper should either provide a principled way to choose Δ^{(N)} that preserves the ordered activation, or acknowledge that the last neuron's activation may deviate from the ideal pattern and discuss the impact on interpolation accuracy. Add a justification for the choice of Δ^{(N)} or a note about the resulting approximation error.

comments.title: Lemma 1 proof assumes distinct inputs not guaranteed
comments.quote: Proof: Let $N_{1}=(x^{<1>}=x^{(1)},x^{<2>},\ldots,x^{<n_{1}>})$; note that $x^{<k>}$ is not necessarily $x^{(k)}$ except for $k=1$ for initialization. To prove the lemma, take a sample $(x,y)\in N_{1}\times\alpha_{1}$. Then we must have $x=x^{<j>},y=\alpha_{1}^{<j>}$ for some $j=1,\ldots,n_{1}$. Since $[v_{1}]_{j^{\prime}}=\sigma_{dsa}(\|x-\eta_{1}^{<j^{\prime}>}\|)$ and $\eta_{1}^{<j^{\prime}>}=x^{<j^{\prime}>}$ for all $j^{\prime}=1,\ldots,n_{1}$, we get exactly $[v_{1}]_{j}=1$. Furthermore, for other $i\neq j$, we have $[v_{1}]_{i}<1$ due to the admission conditions (1) and (2) used during *check* $N_{1}$ *process*.
comments.explanation:
The proof claims that for any sample $(x,y)\in N_1	imes\alpha_1$, we have $[v_1]_j=1$ for the corresponding neuron $j$ and $[v_1]_i<1$ for $i
eq j$. However, the admission conditions (1) and (2) only guarantee that when a sample is *admitted* to layer 1, its activation values on existing neurons are either all below $	au_{ad}$ (case 1) or at least one above $	au_{act}$ (case 2). They do *not* guarantee that for a sample already stored as a neuron, its activation on *other* stored neurons is strictly less than 1. It is possible that two distinct training samples are identical in input (e.g., duplicate rows in the dataset), in which case $\|x-\eta_1^{<i>}\|=0$ for $i
eq j$, giving $[v_1]_i=1$ as well, violating the strict inequality. The paper does not exclude duplicate inputs, and the construction does not prevent them from being admitted as separate neurons. The claim that $[v_1]_i<1$ for all $i
eq j$ is therefore not guaranteed by the stated conditions. Rewrite the proof to either assume all $x^{<j>}$ are distinct, or handle the duplicate case separately (e.g., by noting that if duplicates exist, the argmax still retrieves the correct $y$ because all matching neurons give the same output).

comments.title: Layer k neuron definition has circular dependency
comments.quote: Initialize by first checking $v_{k}^{<1>}$, the activation of $u^{<1>}$ at layer $k$ for collision; if collision occurs, see next paragraph, otherwise, set $N_{k}=(\eta_{k}^{<1>}),\alpha_{k}=(y_{k}^{<1>})$ i.e. $\eta_{k}^{<1>}\leftarrow v_{k}^{<1>}$.
comments.explanation: In layer 1, neurons store the input samples themselves ($\eta_1^{<j>}=x^{<j>}$). In layer $k>1$, the construction says neurons store the activation vector $v_k^{<i>}$ of the sample at that layer. However, the activation $v_k^{<i>}$ is defined as $[v_k^{<i>}]_j = \sigma_{dsa}(\|v_{k-1} - \eta_k^{<j>}\|)$, which depends on the *current* layer's neurons $\eta_k^{<j>}$. At initialization, $\eta_k^{<1>}$ is set to $v_k^{<1>}$, but $v_k^{<1>}$ itself is computed using the (as yet empty) layer $k$? The paper does not specify how $v_k^{<1>}$ is computed before any neurons exist in layer $k$. Typically, one would compute the activation of the sample at layer $k$ by propagating through layers $1,\ldots,k-1$ and then using the (empty) layer $k$ as a pass-through or identity, but this is not defined. This creates a circular dependency: to compute $v_k^{<1>}$, we need $\eta_k$, but to define $\eta_k$, we need $v_k^{<1>}$. The authors should clarify how the activation $v_k^{<1>}$ is computed for the first sample in a new layer, or revise the construction to initialize layer $k$ with the propagated activation from the previous layer (i.e., the vector $v_{k-1}$ itself) rather than the activation at layer $k$.

comments.title: Interpolation formula uses mismatched layer indices
comments.quote: Suppose $V_{1}=[v_{m}]_{i}$ and $V_{2}=[v_{n}]_{j}$ are the two most activated neurons, then the interpolated value can be, for example, $y=\frac{V_{1}[\alpha_{m}]_{i}+V_{2}[\alpha_{n}]_{j}}{V_{1}+V_{2}}$.
comments.explanation: The interpolation formula uses $[v_m]_i$ and $[v_n]_j$ as activation values, but these are scalars (the activation of a single neuron). However, $[\alpha_m]_i$ and $[\alpha_n]_j$ are the output values associated with those neurons. The notation $[v_m]_i$ is ambiguous: earlier, $v_k$ is defined as a vector of activations at layer $k$, so $[v_m]_i$ is the activation of the $i$-th neuron in layer $m$. This is consistent. However, the formula implicitly assumes that the two most activated neurons come from potentially different layers ($m$ and $n$). The paper does not specify how to compare activations across layers (e.g., are they normalized?). Without normalization, a neuron in layer 1 might have activation 0.8 while a neuron in layer 2 might have activation 0.9, but these values are not directly comparable because they are computed from different inputs (original $x$ vs. propagated $v_{k-1}$). The interpolation method should either restrict to neurons within the same layer or define a cross-layer normalization scheme. Add a clarification that activations are compared only within the same layer, or provide a method to make them comparable across layers.

comments.title: Retraining conflates generalizability with data leakage
comments.quote: SQANN is tested for regression on Boston Housing and Diabetes Datasets to demonstrate its generalizability to unseen/test samples, as the following (simplified here): (1) A small subset of samples  $D \subseteq \mathcal{D}$  (the first  $20\%$  of full dataset  $\mathcal{D}$ ) is used to train SQANN and 9 other regression methods. (2) Mean Squared Errors (MSE) values are measured on unused data  $D_{test}$  on all 10 models; we expect large errors on some test samples. (3) SQANN's activations are used to collect samples with large absolute errors  $e_{\tau}(x) = |SQANN(x) - y_0| > \tau$  and we treat them as out-of-distribution (OOD) samples. These samples are considered as new distinct samples to be integrated into  $D$  as the new training dataset  $D'$ . (4) Train the 10 models, now with  $D'$ . (5) Then MSE is measured again on  $D_{test}$  (yes, there will be partial overfitting). From Boston dataset: for  $\tau = 5$ , SQANN MSE improved from 9.90 to 3.08. Decision tree improves the most with  $D'$  (7.36 to 2.07); see table 1.
comments.explanation: The experiment design does not support a claim of generalizability. Step (3) selects test samples with large errors and adds them to the training set; step (5) then measures MSE on the same test set, which now partially overlaps with training. This is an active learning or data incorporation procedure, not a test of generalization to unseen data. The authors acknowledge 'partial overfitting' but do not correct for it, so the numerical improvement (9.90 to 3.08) is uninterpretable as evidence of generalizability. Rewrite the experiment to use a truly held-out test set that is never used for training, or clarify that this is a demonstration of active learning / data incorporation, not generalizability.

comments.title: Pseudocode contains syntax errors and is incomplete
comments.explanation: The pseudocode contains multiple syntax errors and undefined variables that make it unexecutable and unclear. For example: '1-now' is not a valid variable name (starts with a digit); 'ssg' is used but only 'ssig' is defined; '1.j' is not valid; '1.c' is not defined; 'return_index' and 'push_node' are not defined; the indentation is inconsistent. Additionally, the pseudocode is incomplete—it does not show how layers are built or how the final output is computed. The authors should provide a complete, syntactically correct pseudocode or remove it entirely, as it does not add clarity. A proper algorithmic description in the main text would be more helpful.

comments.title: Double selective activation definition has unspecified parameter n
comments.quote:
Double selective activation. Given selective activation $\pi(x,a)=\frac{a}{a+x^{2}}$ and Super Gaussian $s_{g}(x,a)=exp(-(x/a)^{2n})$, $n=4$,$r=0.5$, then the double selective activation is (fig. 3(A)):

$\sigma_{dsa}(x,a_{1},a_{2},r)=(1-r)\times\pi(x,a_{1})+r\times s_{g}(x,a_{2})$ (4)
comments.explanation: The definition of the Super Gaussian function $s_g(x,a)=exp(-(x/a)^{2n})$ includes an unspecified parameter $n$ that is set to 4 in the text. However, the function $\sigma_{dsa}$ is defined with parameters $a_1, a_2, r$ but does not include $n$ as a parameter, even though $n$ affects the shape of the Super Gaussian component. This is a minor inconsistency: either $n$ should be included as a parameter of $\sigma_{dsa}$, or the text should clarify that $n$ is fixed (e.g., $n=4$) and not a tunable parameter. Add $n$ to the parameter list of $\sigma_{dsa}$ or state that $n$ is a fixed constant.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes two novel architectures, TNN and SQANN, designed for interpretability and universal approximation. TNN relies on linearly ordered inputs to create a step-function activation pattern, while SQANN attempts to extend this to higher dimensions using a 'fingerprint' activation mechanism. The work is conceptually interesting, particularly the focus on identifying training samples via activation patterns. However, the paper is hindered by a significant misalignment between its title/claims and the actual mathematical scope of the models, as well as internal contradictions regarding the mechanism of catastrophic forgetting. The theoretical proofs are limited to training accuracy rather than generalization, and the experimental section lacks a rigorous test of the models' behavior on truly out-of-distribution data.

The paper makes a valiant attempt to bridge the gap between interpretable models and universal approximation, but it falls short of establishing a novel contribution to the field. The TNN model is essentially a specialized interpolation method for 1D time series, not a general universal approximator. The SQANN model introduces complex layer destruction logic that contradicts its own claims of resistance to catastrophic forgetting. The lack of generalization analysis and complexity benchmarks makes the work incomplete for a top-tier venue.

Overview issues:

- Misalignment of Title and Model Scope: The title claims the paper presents 'Universal Approximations,' but the TNN model is strictly restricted to linearly ordered inputs ($x^{(1)} > x^{(2)} > 	ext{...}$). This assumption severely limits the model to 1D time series data and prevents it from approximating functions on general compact sets in $	ext{R}^n$. A universal approximator should handle arbitrary inputs. The paper must either rename the contribution to reflect this 1D limitation or extend the TNN construction to handle unordered, multi-dimensional inputs.

- Internal Contradiction in Catastrophic Forgetting Claims: The abstract and introduction claim the models are 'resistant to catastrophic forgetting.' However, the SQANN construction mechanism (Section 3.1) explicitly destroys layers ($l > l_c$) and reconstructs them when a collision occurs. If the model structure is altered, the weights representing the old training data are overwritten. The authors argue that forgetting is avoided because 'each training point... is still identified with a neuron,' but this ignores the fact that the neuron's weights have changed due to the layer destruction. This contradiction invalidates the main theoretical claim of the paper.

- Misinterpretation of Universal Approximation: The paper cites the Universal Approximation Theorem (Cybenko, 1989) but claims 'arbitrarily high accuracy on training dataset' as a result. This is trivial for any interpolator and does not constitute universal approximation. Universal approximation implies the ability to approximate a continuous function on a compact set, which requires generalization to unseen inputs. The proofs provided (Theorem 1, Theorem 2) only establish interpolation accuracy on discrete training points. The contribution is better described as 'Interpolating Memory-Based Networks' rather than 'Universal Approximators.'

- Lack of Generalization to Truly Unseen Data: The experiments (Section 3.2) only test generalization by adding noise (spread) to training points, not by introducing data from a different distribution. The 'universal approximation' theorems do not establish generalization; they only establish interpolation. Given that TNN and SQANN are essentially interpolation engines, they will fail on inputs far from the convex hull of the training samples. The paper is missing a critical analysis or experiment demonstrating the model's behavior on truly out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs, which is essential for any 'universal' claim.

- Missing Computational Complexity Analysis: The paper proposes complex mechanisms like 'layer destruction' and 'collision resolution' but does not analyze the computational cost or memory footprint. TNN has $O(N)$ parameters, but SQANN could have $O(N^2)$ or worse due to the recursive nature of the construction and the potential for deep layers. Without a complexity analysis comparing SQANN to standard neural networks (MLP) and Radial Basis Functions (RBFs), the reader cannot assess the practical utility of the model. A section analyzing time and space complexity is required.

Recommendation: Major Revision. The core ideas have potential for interpretability, but the theoretical claims are overstated and the experimental validation is insufficient. The paper needs to clarify the scope (1D vs. general), fix the contradiction in the forgetting mechanism, and add experiments on true generalization.

Revision targets:

- Correct the scope of the title and TNN section to reflect the restriction to linearly ordered inputs (1D).

- Resolve the contradiction in the SQANN construction regarding catastrophic forgetting by either making the mechanism non-destructive or removing the forgetting claim.

- Add experiments or theoretical analysis demonstrating generalization to inputs outside the convex hull of the training set (true OOD data).

- Include a computational complexity analysis comparing SQANN to standard neural networks and RBFs.
